This Pharisee, as well as others, seems to have had a bad plan when he invited Jesus to his house. But our Lord would not be stopped from healing a man, even though he knew people would complain about him doing it on the Sabbath. It requires careful thought to understand how piety and charity fit together in observing the Sabbath, and the difference between works of real necessity and habits of self-indulgence. Wisdom from above teaches patient perseverance in doing good.

Even in everyday actions, Christ notices what we do, not only in our religious gatherings but at our tables. We often see that a person's pride will bring them down, and humility comes before honor. Our Savior here teaches that works of charity are better than acts of show. But our Lord did not mean that proud and unbelieving generosity should be rewarded, but that his instruction to do good to the poor and afflicted should be followed from love for him.

In this parable, observe the free grace and mercy of God shining in the gospel of Christ, which will be food and a feast for the soul of a person who knows their own needs and miseries. Everyone found some excuse to put off attending. This rebukes the Jewish nation for their neglect of the offers of Christ’s grace. It also shows the reluctance there is to accept the gospel call. The lack of gratitude in those who reject gospel offers, and the contempt shown toward the God of heaven thereby, justly provokes him. The apostles were to turn to the Gentiles when the Jews refused the offer; and with them the church was filled. The provision made for precious souls in the gospel of Christ has not been made in vain; for if some reject it, others will thankfully accept the offer. The very poor and those who are low in the world will be as welcome to Christ as the rich and great; and often the gospel has the greatest success among those who suffer from worldly disadvantages and physical weakness. Christ’s house will at last be filled; it will be so when the number of the elect is completed.

Although the disciples of Christ are not all crucified, they all bear their cross, and must bear it in the way of duty. Jesus tells them to expect this, and then consider it. Our Savior explains this with two examples: one showing that we must consider the expenses of our religion; the other, that we must consider the dangers of it. Sit down and count the cost; consider that it will cost you to put an end to sin, even your most cherished desires. The proudest and most daring sinner cannot stand against God, for who knows the power of his anger? It is in our best interest to seek peace with him, and we need not ask what conditions are needed for peace, they are offered to us, and are highly beneficial. In some way a disciple of Christ will be put to the test. May we strive to be true disciples, and be careful not to weaken in our profession or be afraid of the cross; that we may be the good salt of the earth, to season those around us with the flavor of Christ.
